nativeRange.habitat: Riversides, pond edges, and seasonally wet marshy areas in the southeastern United States

summary: A fine-leaved North American stem plant native to the southeastern United States, prized for needle-like foliage that flushes brilliant red to orange-red under high light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization. Works as a colourful background or upper-midground accent in planted tanks.
careNotes: Needle-Leaf Ludwigia (Ludwigia arcuata) is a North American stem plant in the Onagraceae family, native to the southeastern United States. It is recognised in the hobby for its very narrow, linear, slightly arching leaves and the intense red-to-orange coloration it develops when grown submerged. The defining variable in its care is light combined with iron: medium light is sufficient to keep the plant alive, but leaves tend to remain green or orange under those conditions. High light paired with CO2 injection and iron-rich micronutrient dosing is what produces the vivid red coloration the species is typically sold for. CO2 supplementation is technically optional — the plant can survive without it — but is effectively required for deep colour and the dense, compact growth that makes it most useful as an aquascape accent. The plant tends to be fragile and sensitive to drastic parameter swings, which can trigger melt or stem rot; this is especially common with freshly planted tissue-culture material as it transitions from emersed to submersed growth. Planting in groups of 6 or more stems allows it to read as a bushy red accent rather than a collection of scattered single stems. Emersed growth produces rounder, greener leaves, while the narrow needle-leaf form typically seen in aquarium photos is the submersed habit.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: trim healthy tops or lateral side shoots and replant directly into nutrient-rich substrate. The cut mother stem will usually branch and produce multiple new lateral shoots, allowing dense red bushes to be built up over time from a small starter group.
